(**JP**) is a [judicial officer](/wiki/Judicial_officer "Judicial officer") of a [lower court](/wiki/Lower_court "Lower court"), elected or appointed by means of a commission ([letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent")) to keep the peace. In past centuries the term **commissioner of the peace** was often used with the same meaning. Depending on the [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), such justices dispense [summary justice](/wiki/Summary_offence "Summary offence") or merely deal with local administrative applications in [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") jurisdictions. Justices of the peace are appointed or elected from the citizens of the jurisdiction in which they serve, and are (or were) usually not required to have any formal [legal education](/wiki/Legal_education "Legal education") in order to qualify for the office. Some jurisdictions have varying forms of training for JPs. In some jurisdictions, the court over which a justice of the peace presides is called a **justice court**.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Judicial officer

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Taos_County%2C_New_Mexico._Justice_of_the_Peace_Montoyo_hears_a_minor_case._-_NARA_-_522002.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Taos_County%2C_New_Mexico._Justice_of_the_Peace_Montoyo_hears_a_minor_case._-_NARA_-_522002.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taos_County,_New_Mexico._Justice_of_the_Peace_Montoyo_hears_a_minor_case._-_NARA_-_522002.tif)

A justice of the peace in [Taos County, New Mexico](/wiki/Taos_County,_New_Mexico "Taos County, New Mexico"), United States, hears a case (1941).

A **justice of the peace** (**JP**) is a [judicial officer](/wiki/Judicial_officer "Judicial officer") of a [lower court](/wiki/Lower_court "Lower court"), elected or appointed by means of a commission ([letters patent](/wiki/Letters_patent "Letters patent")) to keep the peace. In past centuries the term **commissioner of the peace** was often used with the same meaning. Depending on the [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), such justices dispense [summary justice](/wiki/Summary_offence "Summary offence") or merely deal with local administrative applications in [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") jurisdictions. Justices of the peace are appointed or elected from the citizens of the jurisdiction in which they serve, and are (or were) usually not required to have any formal [legal education](/wiki/Legal_education "Legal education") in order to qualify for the office. Some jurisdictions have varying forms of training for JPs. In some jurisdictions, the court over which a justice of the peace presides is called a **justice court**.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## History in the United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History in the United Kingdom")]

In 1195, [Richard I](/wiki/Richard_I_of_England "Richard I of England") of England and his minister [Hubert Walter](/wiki/Hubert_Walter "Hubert Walter") commissioned certain [knights](/wiki/Knight "Knight") to preserve the peace in unruly areas. They were responsible to the King in ensuring that the law was upheld and preserving the "[King's peace](/wiki/Queen%27s_peace "Queen's peace")". Therefore, they were known as "keepers of the peace".[[2]](#cite_note-2)

An act of 1327 had referred to "good and lawful men" to be appointed in every county in the land to "guard the peace"; such individuals were first referred to as [conservators of the peace](/wiki/Conservator_of_the_peace "Conservator of the peace"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) or wardens of the peace. The title *justice of the peace* derives from 1361,[[4]](#cite_note-4) in the reign of [Edward III](/wiki/Edward_III_of_England "Edward III of England"). The "peace" to be guarded is the sovereign's, the maintenance of which is the duty of the [Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown") under the [royal prerogative](/wiki/Royal_prerogative "Royal prerogative"). Justices of the peace still use the power conferred or re-conferred on them since 1361 to [bind over](/wiki/Bind_over "Bind over") unruly persons "to be of good behaviour". The bind over is not a punishment, but a preventive measure, intended to ensure that people thought likely to offend will not do so. The justices' alternative title of "[magistrate](/wiki/Magistrate "Magistrate")" dates from the 16th century, although the word had been in use centuries earlier to describe some legal officials of [Roman](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome") times.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In the centuries from the [Tudor](/wiki/Tudor_dynasty "Tudor dynasty") period until the onset of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), the JPs constituted a major element of the English (later British) governmental system, which in modern times has sometimes been termed a [squirearchy](/wiki/Squirearchy "Squirearchy") (i.e., dominance of the land-owning [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry")). For example, historian [Tim Blanning](/wiki/Tim_Blanning "Tim Blanning") notes[[6]](#cite_note-6) that while in Britain the royal prerogative was decisively curbed by the [Bill of Rights 1689](/wiki/Bill_of_Rights_1689 "Bill of Rights 1689"), in practice the central government in London had a greater ability to get its policies implemented in the rural outlying regions than could contemporary [absolute monarchies](/wiki/Absolute_monarchies "Absolute monarchies") such as France – a paradox due especially to JPs belonging to the same social class as the Members of Parliament and thus having a direct interest in getting laws actually enforced and implemented on the ground.

Being an unpaid office, undertaken voluntarily and sometimes more for the sake of renown or to confirm the justice's standing within the community, the justice was typically a member of the [gentry](/wiki/Gentry "Gentry"). The justices of the peace conducted [arraignments](/wiki/Arraignment "Arraignment") in all criminal cases, and tried [misdemeanours](/wiki/Misdemeanour "Misdemeanour") and [infractions](/wiki/Infraction "Infraction") of [local ordinances](/wiki/Local_ordinance "Local ordinance") and [bylaws](/wiki/Bylaw "Bylaw"). Towns and boroughs with enough burdensome judicial business that could not find volunteers for the unpaid role of justice of the peace had to petition the Crown for authority to hire a paid [stipendiary magistrate](/wiki/Stipendiary_magistrate "Stipendiary magistrate").

The [Municipal Corporations Act 1835](/wiki/Municipal_Corporations_Act_1835 "Municipal Corporations Act 1835") stripped the power to appoint normal JPs from those municipal corporations that had it. This was replaced by the present system, where the [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") nominates candidates with local advice, for appointment by the [Crown](/wiki/British_monarchy "British monarchy").

Until the introduction of elected [county councils](/wiki/County_council "County council") in the 19th century, JPs, in [quarter sessions](/wiki/Quarter_sessions "Quarter sessions"), also administered the county at a local level. Their many roles included regulating wages and food supplies, managing roads, bridges, prisons and workhouses and they undertook to provide and supervise locally those services mandated by the Crown and Parliament for the welfare of the county. To this end they set the County Rate, where one was set at all.

Women were not allowed to become JPs in the United Kingdom until 1919, the first woman being [Ada Summers](/wiki/Ada_Summers "Ada Summers"), the Mayor of [Stalybridge](/wiki/Stalybridge "Stalybridge"), who was a JP by virtue of her office. In October 1920 Summers was appointed a JP in her own right, alongside other pioneers including [Edith Sutton](/wiki/Edith_Sutton "Edith Sutton")[[7]](#cite_note-7) and [Miriam Lightowler](/wiki/Miriam_Lightowler "Miriam Lightowler") OBE in Halifax.[[8]](#cite_note-8) [Emily Murphy](/wiki/Emily_Murphy "Emily Murphy") of Edmonton, Canada, preceded her by some three and a half years.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) As at 2018 in England and Wales, about one-third of JPs are women.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In special circumstances, a justice of the peace can be the highest governmental representative, so in fact 'gubernatorial', in a colonial entity. This was the case in the [Tati Concessions Land](/wiki/Tati_Concessions_Land "Tati Concessions Land"), a gold-mining [concession](/wiki/Concession_(territory) "Concession (territory)") in the [Matabele kingdom](/wiki/Matabeleland "Matabeleland"), until its annexation by the [British Bechuanaland](/wiki/British_Bechuanaland "British Bechuanaland") protectorate.

## Modern use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Modern use")]

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Australia")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Justice_of_the_peace "Special:EditPage/Justice of the peace") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(December 2024)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A justice of the peace in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") is typically someone of good stature in the community who is authorised to witness and sign [statutory declarations](/wiki/Statutory_declaration "Statutory declaration") and [affidavits](/wiki/Affidavit "Affidavit") and to certify copies of original documents.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

There are no Federal level JPs in Australia, as this power is retained by the State Governments and devolved to the Territory Governments. Where a Federal Government document or task requires the services of a JP, the rules of each individual State or Territory government will dictate if they have the authority to assist. Criteria for appointment vary widely, depending on the state.

#### Australian Capital Territory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Australian Capital Territory")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Justice_of_the_peace "Special:EditPage/Justice of the peace") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(April 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In the [Australian Capital Territory](/wiki/Australian_Capital_Territory "Australian Capital Territory") (colloquially, the ACT), there is only the single level of Justice of the Peace. They are appointed on an as-needed basis, and a potential appointee must be an Australian Citizen, and both a resident of, and enrolled on the electoral roll, of the territory. They must also not be an undischarged bankrupt, and consent to criminal history checks being undertaken prior to appointment.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Appointment is for life, unless a JP resigns, is suspended/dismissed from office, or resides outside of the ACT for a period of more than 12 consecutive months.

JPs for the ACT also cover the Australian External Territory of [Norfolk Island](/wiki/Norfolk_Island "Norfolk Island"), and the Internal [Jervis Bay Territory](/wiki/Jervis_Bay_Territory "Jervis Bay Territory"), subject to local law variations in those two jurisdictions.

#### New South Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: New South Wales")]

The most common functions performed by a justice of the peace in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") are to witness the signing of a statutory declaration,[[14]](#cite_note-NSWstatdec-14) witness the signing of an affidavit and certify that a copy of an original document is a true copy.[[15]](#cite_note-jp.nsw.gov.au-15)

JPs are appointed by the [Governor of New South Wales](/wiki/Governor_of_New_South_Wales "Governor of New South Wales") for five-year terms. They are volunteers, who come from all walks of life and all sections of the community. JPs are people who are trusted to be honest, careful and impartial when performing the functions of a JP. They must not charge a fee or accept a gift for providing JP services, tell people what to write in a statutory declaration,[[16]](#cite_note-NSW-16) write it for them, or give them legal advice.[[15]](#cite_note-jp.nsw.gov.au-15)

Ways to find a JP in New South Wales include:

:   1. Search the JP Public Register, which lists all JPs for each postcode area and provides a telephone contact number for JPs who serve the community directly.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

:   2. Check a public listing of scheduled JP services to find when JPs are available at scheduled times and locations across the state.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[*[dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*]

In the early years of the [Colony of New South Wales](/wiki/Colony_of_New_South_Wales "Colony of New South Wales"), justices of the peace had far greater responsibilities and broader roles in the administration of justice than now.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

#### Queensland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Queensland")]

In the state of [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland "Queensland"), a "justice of the peace (qualified)" has the additional powers to issue search warrants and arrest warrants and, in conjunction with another justice of the peace (qualified) constitute a [magistrates' court](/wiki/Magistrates_Court_of_Queensland "Magistrates Court of Queensland") for exercising powers to remand defendants in custody, grant bail, and adjourn court hearings.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Some justices are appointed as justice of the peace (magistrates' court), usually in remote [Aboriginal](/wiki/Aboriginal_Australians "Aboriginal Australians") communities, to perform many of the functions that might otherwise fall to a stipendiary [magistrate](/wiki/Magistrate "Magistrate").

In Queensland, a lawyer may be appointed as a Justice of the Peace without further education or qualification and has the full powers of a JP (Magistrate's Court). A commissioner for declarations (C.dec) has powers limited to witnessing documents, witnessing statutory declarations, witnessing affidavits, witnessing and administering oaths and affirmations.[[20]](#cite_note-:0-20)

The first woman to become a JP in Queensland was Matilda (Maud) Hennessey of [Mackay](/wiki/Mackay,_Queensland "Mackay, Queensland") on 24 April 1918.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

#### South Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: South Australia")]

In South Australia, there are two types of justices: justice of the peace and special justices.

A justice of the peace in South Australia is typically someone of good stature in the community who is authorised to witness and sign [statutory declarations](/wiki/Statutory_declaration "Statutory declaration"), [affidavits](/wiki/Affidavit "Affidavit"), [waiver rights](/wiki/Waiver "Waiver"), [search warrants](/wiki/Search_warrants "Search warrants"), drug warrants, divorce documents, and to certify copies of original documents and to witness the signing of [power of attorney](/wiki/Power_of_attorney "Power of attorney") and guardianship documents, providing the JP is satisfied with the capability of the signatory.

A Special Justice (SJ) is a higher level of justice of the peace in South Australia; they sit on the bench of the [magistrates' court](/wiki/Magistrates%27_Court_of_South_Australia "Magistrates' Court of South Australia") hearing cases in the petty sessions division.

The South Australian Attorney-General has set up a web site to locate justices of the peace.[[25]](#cite_note-25) The majority of metropolitan and many regional Councils (Local Government authorities) have a rotational justice of the peace in residence at nominated times.

South Australia's first women justices were appointed in July 1915.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

#### Victoria

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Victoria")]

Justices of the peace and bail justices, who are also volunteers, are appointed to serve a semi-judicial function in all areas of the Victorian community. The main official roles in the Victorian community include witnessing statutory declarations, witnessing affidavits and hearing bail matters outside court hours (bail justices only).[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-:1-28)

The first woman to become a JP in Victoria was [Mary Catherine Rogers](/wiki/Mary_Rogers_(politician) "Mary Rogers (politician)") who, in 1920, became the first woman councillor in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") when she was elected in the [City of Richmond](/wiki/City_of_Richmond "City of Richmond").[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Justices of the peace provide a service to the community as independent witnesses of statutory declarations, powers of attorney and affidavits. JPs, who are also volunteers, are selected through an extensive interview, written exam and practical testing. They are recommended by the state attorney-general and appointed by the governor-in-council, and it is their job to authorise and witness statutory declarations and affidavits within the state of Victoria. As of August 2022, there are currently around 3500 JPs and bail justices in Victoria, who collectively sign more than 1.5 million documents and assist more than 350,000 people each year.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Justices of the Peace and Bail Justices may use the [post-nominals](/wiki/Post-nominal "Post-nominal") JP and BJ respectively after their names.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

##### Bail justices

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Bail justices")]

The primary role of a bail justice within Victoria is to hear bail applications (including after-hours bail) under the Bail Act 1977,[[32]](#cite_note-32) and to hear applications for Interim Accommodation Orders for children (under the Children, Youth and Families Act 2005).[[33]](#cite_note-33) Bail justices can also witness Victorian statutory declarations and affidavits.[[28]](#cite_note-:1-28) Bail justices are appointed for terms of four years and may be re-appointed repeatedly until they attain 70 years of age.[[34]](#cite_note-34) They are often required to attend call outs and rule on bail applications or protection applications for children in danger on weekends and late at night when the courts are closed.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Candidates must successfully complete a three-day training course run by the Department of Justice. Bail justices, also have some limited powers under federal legislation, including the power to conduct interstate extradition hearings and extending question time for federal police.

#### Western Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Western Australia")]

Justices of the peace in [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia") are appointed by the Governor who authorises them to carry out a wide range of official administrative and judicial duties in the community.

As well as presiding in the Magistrates Court, justices of the peace are regularly called upon by the WA Police to sign search warrants and authorise the issuing of summonses. The administrative tasks include witnessing affidavits and documents such as wills and statutory declarations.

"Visiting justices" are a special group of justices of the peace, appointed to preside over cases within the prison system.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

JPs for Western Australia also cover the Australian External Territories of [Cocos (Keeling) Islands](/wiki/Cocos_(Keeling)_Islands "Cocos (Keeling) Islands") and [Christmas Island](/wiki/Christmas_Island "Christmas Island").

### Bangladesh

In virtue of their respective offices, the Judges of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Bangladesh "Supreme Court of Bangladesh") are Justices of the Peace within and for of the whole of [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh"), Sessions Judges, Chief Judicial Magistrate and Metropolitan Magistrates are Justices of the Peace within their respective jurisdictions.

[The Government](/wiki/The_Government "The Government") may, by notification in the official Gazette, appoint such persons resident within [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and not being the subjects of any foreign State as it thinks fit to be Justices of the Peace within and for the local area mentioned in such notification.

### Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Belgium")]

Main article: [Justice of the peace (Belgium)](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace_(Belgium) "Justice of the peace (Belgium)")

In [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), the justices of the peace ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *vredegerecht*, [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *justice de paix*, [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Friedensgericht*) function as the [small claims courts](/wiki/Small_claims_court "Small claims court") in the [country's judicial system](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Belgium "Judiciary of Belgium"); they stand at the bottom of the Belgian judicial hierarchy and only handle civil cases. There is a justice of the peace in each [judicial canton of Belgium](/wiki/Cantons_of_Belgium "Cantons of Belgium"), of which there are 187 in total as of 2017. The justices of the peace have [original jurisdiction](/wiki/Original_jurisdiction "Original jurisdiction") over cases in which the disputed amount does not exceed 5,000 euro (as of September 2018), except for the [matters](/wiki/Subject-matter_jurisdiction "Subject-matter jurisdiction") over which another court or tribunal has [exclusive jurisdiction](/wiki/Exclusive_jurisdiction "Exclusive jurisdiction"). In addition, the justices of the peace have original jurisdiction over a number of matters irrespective of the disputed amount, such as cases involving the [renting](/wiki/Renting "Renting") or [leasing](/wiki/Lease "Lease") of [real estate](/wiki/Real_estate "Real estate"), [evictions](/wiki/Eviction "Eviction"), [easement](/wiki/Easement "Easement"), [land consolidation](/wiki/Land_consolidation "Land consolidation"), [consumer credit](/wiki/Credit "Credit") or unpaid [utility bills](/wiki/Utility_bill "Utility bill"). The justices of the peace also have original jurisdiction in certain aspects of [family law](/wiki/Family_law "Family law"), most notably [legal guardianships](/wiki/Legal_guardian "Legal guardian") for incapacitated seniors, and the [involuntary commitment](/wiki/Involuntary_commitment "Involuntary commitment") of the mentally ill to [psychiatric facilities](/wiki/Psychiatric_hospital "Psychiatric hospital"). The judgments made by the justices of the peace can, with some exceptions, be appealed to the [tribunals of first instance](/wiki/Tribunal_of_first_instance_(Belgium) "Tribunal of first instance (Belgium)").[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Canada")]

In Canada, justices of the peace play a role in the administration of justice at the provincial level. Justices are generally appointed by the [lieutenant governors of Canada's provinces](/wiki/Lieutenant-Governor_(Canada) "Lieutenant-Governor (Canada)"), and by the [commissioners of Canada's territories](/wiki/Commissioner#Canadian_territories "Commissioner"), on the [advice](/wiki/Advice_(constitutional) "Advice (constitutional)") of their relevant [premier](/wiki/First_minister#Canada "First minister") or [Attorney General](/wiki/Attorney_General "Attorney General"). Canada made the second (first was in South Australia a year earlier) appointment in the then British Empire of a woman as a magistrate, namely [Emily Murphy](/wiki/Emily_Murphy "Emily Murphy"), who was sworn in as a police magistrate in the Women's Court of the City of Edmonton (Alberta) on 19 June 1916.

#### British Columbia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: British Columbia")]

In [British Columbia](/wiki/British_Columbia "British Columbia"), pursuant to the *Provincial Court Act*, all judges are justices of the peace, and hence all of them are [peace officers](/wiki/Peace_officer "Peace officer").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Northwest Territories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Northwest Territories")]

In the [Northwest Territories](/wiki/Northwest_Territories "Northwest Territories"), justices may hear summary conviction matters, municipal by-laws, and certain criminal matters.[[40]](#cite_note-40) However, in more populated provinces justices usually preside over bail hearings and provincial offences courts. When not in a court session, a justice can perform other judicial functions, such as issuing search warrants.

#### Ontario

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Ontario")]

In [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), justices of the peace can preside over judicial interim release (bail) hearings and other criminal hearings. JPs can also exercise jurisdiction over provincial regulatory offences and municipal by-law prosecutions. JPs must retire by reaching the age of 65, but may continue working until 75 subject to the approval of the [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_justice "Chief justice") of the [Ontario Court of Justice](/wiki/Ontario_Court_of_Justice "Ontario Court of Justice").[[41]](#cite_note-41)

#### Quebec

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Quebec")]

In Quebec, there are two type of justices of the peace, administrative justice of the peace and presiding justice of the peace.

Administrative justice of the peace are court officers appointed by the [Minister of Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(Quebec) "Ministry of Justice (Quebec)"), and perform duties such as receiving criminal informations and issuing warrants. Presiding justice of the peace are appointed by commission under the [Great Seal](/wiki/Great_Seal_of_Quebec "Great Seal of Quebec"), and can try some criminal matters and issue warrants. They are appointed from advocates of at least ten years' standing and serve full-time until the age of 70.

#### Yukon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Yukon")]

In Yukon, justices of the peace are lay officers of the court. They sit in the Justice of the Peace Court, which is part of the [Territorial Court of Yukon](/wiki/Territorial_Court_of_Yukon "Territorial Court of Yukon").

### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

In Hong Kong, the historical functions of justices of the peace have been replaced by full-time, legally qualified [magistrates](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Hong_Kong#The_Magistrates'_Courts "Judiciary of Hong Kong"). Nowadays, justices of the peace are essentially titles of honour given by the Government to community leaders, and to certain officials while they are in their terms of offices. They have no judicial functions, and their main duties include visiting prisons, institutions for young offenders and drug addicts, psychiatric hospitals, remand homes, places of refuge, reception and detention centres,[[42]](#cite_note-42) administering statutory declarations, and serving as members of advisory panels. They also monitor the drawing of the [Mark Six](/wiki/Mark_Six#Fairness "Mark Six") to ensure fairness.

### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: India")]

In India, justices of the peace exist,[[43]](#cite_note-43) but no longer occupy a prominent post. One of the famous justices in India was [Kavasji Jamshedji Petigara](/wiki/Kavasji_Jamshedji_Petigara "Kavasji Jamshedji Petigara").

### Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Ireland")]

'Justices of the peace' existed in Ireland prior to 1922, sitting in a bench under the supervision of [resident magistrates](/wiki/Resident_magistrate "Resident magistrate") at [petty sessions](/wiki/Petty_sessions "Petty sessions") to try minor offences summarily, and with a [county court](/wiki/County_court "County court") judge (in his capacity of chairman of quarter sessions) and jury to try more serious offences at [quarter sessions](/wiki/Quarter_sessions "Quarter sessions"). In the [Irish Free State](/wiki/Irish_Free_State "Irish Free State") the position was effectively abolished by the District Justices (Temporary Provisions) Act 1923[[44]](#cite_note-44) and permanently abolished by the [Courts of Justice Act 1924](/wiki/Courts_of_Justice_Act_1924 "Courts of Justice Act 1924"). Their judicial powers were replaced by full-time, salaried, legally qualified [district justices](/wiki/District_Court_(Ireland) "District Court (Ireland)") (now called district judges) and their quasi-judicial powers by unpaid lay [peace commissioners](/wiki/Peace_Commissioner "Peace Commissioner"). However, the power of Peace Commissioners has been reduced following a number of Supreme Court Challenges. Even one Government Department stopped accepting [Peace Commissioner signatures](https://www.kildarestreet.com/wrans/?id=2013-09-18a.3394&s=peace+commissioner+signature+coveney#g3396.r) because "there is no available updated register or reliable data base to confirm that the person signing the form as a witness is in fact a Peace Commissioner. In the absence of such verification being possible, the practice was changed to remove the risk of fraudulent activity and maintain the integrity of the process." In general, Peace commissioners may sign statutory declarations, and may rarely issue summons and search warrants to the [Garda Síochána](/wiki/Garda_S%C3%ADoch%C3%A1na "Garda Síochána") (Irish police).[[45]](#cite_note-45) A peace commissioner can witness the signature of an affidavit. In addition Peace Commissioners can sign custody agreements between legal guardians in relation to visitation etc. These agreements are legally binding and can be altered only by a Judge in the regular courts. Peace Commissioners are appointed on the basis of good character and usually prominent standing in their local communities.

### Jamaica

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Jamaica")]

A justice of the peace, according to the Ministry of Justice, is a person of unquestionable integrity who seeks to promote and protect the rights of the individual and helps to provide justice to persons in a particular community. Additionally, the JP serves as a justice in petty court sessions, attends juvenile court sessions, issues summonses, considers applications for bail, explains and signs legal documents, sits on licensing panels, and gives counsel/advice. Any [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica") citizen that can speak and write English is eligible to become a JP. Any club/organisation/citizen can recommend someone to become JP for a community. JPs are chosen under the [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_Jamaica "Governor-General of Jamaica")'s discretion.

### Malaysia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Malaysia")]

In [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysia "Malaysia"), justices of the peace (*jaksa pendamai* in [Malay](/wiki/Malay_language "Malay language"), also abbreviated JP) have largely been replaced in magistrates' courts by legally qualified (first-class) stipendiary magistrates. However, state governments continue to appoint justices of the peace as honours. In 2004, some associations of justices of the peace pressed the federal government to allow justices of the peace to sit as second-class magistrates in order to reduce the backlog of cases in the courts.

### New Zealand

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: New Zealand")]

The legal framework for the office of Justice of the Peace within [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") is derived from the Act of Parliament 'Justices of the Peace Act 1957', and subsequent amendments.[[46]](#cite_note-NZJPAct-46)

There are two levels of this position within New Zealand: The standard level of 'Justice of the Peace', and a separate 'Judicial Justice of the Peace'. Persons so appointed may use the post-nominals JP and JJP respectively. A JP who is retired may apply to the [Secretary for Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(New_Zealand) "Ministry of Justice (New Zealand)") for permission to use the post-nominals 'JP (retired)'.[[47]](#cite_note-NZRetirement-47)

Appointment as a JP is for life, unless a voluntary resignation is tendered in writing, or a JP is suspended or dismissed from office due to misconduct, bankruptcy, or other specific reasons.[[48]](#cite_note-NZJPAppt-48)

#### Justice of the Peace

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Justice of the Peace")]

A JP in New Zealand is someone of good stature in the community who is authorised to witness and sign a number of documents, including [statutory declarations](/wiki/Statutory_declaration "Statutory declaration"), [affidavits](/wiki/Affidavit "Affidavit"), and producing [certified copies](/wiki/Certified_copy "Certified copy") of documents, amongst others. In some limited circumstances they may also perform citizenship ceremonies, and act as a 'Visiting Justice' in prisons. They are nominated for office by local Members of Parliament and appointed by the [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand"). They must take both the Oath of Allegiance and the Judicial Oath.

#### Judicial Justice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Judicial Justice")]

If a local JP Federation determines that a need for one or more JJPs exists, they will issue a call for nominations to JPs in their territory. Following closure of nominations, a shortlist will be drawn up, and interviews undertaken. If a prospective applicant passes the interview stage, and the relevant training, exams, and assignments are successfully completed, they can be sworn in as a JJP. This means they can then be assigned to the bench in the relevant District Court to oversee minor criminal cases. These would involve tasks such as the exercise of powers to remand defendants in custody, grant bail, and adjourn court hearings.

Appointment as a JJP is at the pleasure of the Secretary for Justice, generally for a minimum of 5 years, and for as long as there is a need in the area an individual resides. Should an individual move abroad, to an area of New Zealand where the service is no longer required, or the local area no longer requires it, the JJP appointment will be terminated.

#### Issuing Officer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Issuing Officer")]

Prior to 2012, all JPs were able to issue search and arrest warrants, but with the passage of the NZ Act of Parliament "[Search and Surveillance Act 2012](/wiki/Search_and_Surveillance_Act_2012_and_human_rights "Search and Surveillance Act 2012 and human rights")", this power was spun off to the separate position of Issuing Officer.[[49]](#cite_note-SearchSurveillanceAct-49)

Under the law, an Issuing Officer does not have to be a Justice of the Peace - Holders of some positions within the judicial system (e.g. Registrar or Deputy Registrar of a court) are Issuing Officers *[ex officio](/wiki/List_of_Latin_phrases_(E)#ex_officio "List of Latin phrases (E)")* for the duration of their time in those roles.

For a JP or JJP to exercise this power after 2012, they must apply to become an Issuing Officer separately. The application process involves a further training course and exam, followed by a vetting and approval process by the [Attorney-General](/wiki/Attorney-General_(New_Zealand) "Attorney-General (New Zealand)"). Appointment as an Issuing Officer is for a maximum of three years, and may be renewed for a further three years at expiry as needed.

On 1 October 2025, new amendements came into force as part of amendments to the "Mutual Assistance in Criminal Matters Act 1992", in support of New Zealand's accession to the [Budapest Convention on Cybercrime](/wiki/Budapest_Convention_on_Cybercrime "Budapest Convention on Cybercrime"). These amendments mean that some powers to issue search warrants and production orders arising from criminal matters in other countries that were previously available to Issuing Officers, are instead now reserved only to High Court Judges.[[50]](#cite_note-MutualAssistanceinCriminalMattersAct-50)

### Pakistan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Pakistan")]

Sections 22, 22-A and 22-B of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898 provide for the appointment of justices of the peace by the provincial governments, their powers and duties respectively. However, seldom are justices of the peace appointed in Pakistan outside the judiciary. Session and additional session judges act as ex-officio justices of the peace as per Section 25 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1898. An Ex-officio Justice of the Peace may issue appropriate directions to the police authorities concerned on a complaint regarding-
(i) non-registration of a criminal case;
(ii) transfer of investigation from one police officer to another; and
(iii) neglect, failure or excess committed by a police authority in relation to its functions and duties.
Such functions being quasi-judicial in nature could not be termed as executive, administrative or ministerial.(PLD 2016 Supreme Court 581)

It is pertinent to note however, as many academics have pointed out, that there is great utility in the appointment of such justices especially in rural areas where enmity between rival groups can lead to the inability of registration of cognizable offences and biased judicial proceedings.

### Russia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Russia")]

Main article: [Magistrate (Russia)](/wiki/Magistrate_(Russia) "Magistrate (Russia)")

### Singapore

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Singapore")]

In [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore"), the functions of Justices of the Peace have been replaced by full-time, legally qualified [magistrates](/wiki/Judicial_officers_of_the_Republic_of_Singapore#Judicial_officers_of_the_State_Courts "Judicial officers of the Republic of Singapore"). JP in Singapore are appointed amongst outstanding individuals in Singapore who have made significant contributions in their professions, the public service, social services and the community at large by the President of the Republic of Singapore, under section 11(1) of the State Courts Act 1970 for a renewable five-year term.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

JP do not serve any judicial roles, rather they derive their functions from statute.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Some examples of the functions, powers and duties of a Justice of the Peace includes:

* In accordance with the Prisons Act 1933, a JP who is appointed as a Visiting Justice may visit any [Singapore prisons](/wiki/Changi_Prison "Changi Prison") at any time to ascertain whether the prison regulations are adhered to. A JP may also upon receiving a report of an aggravated prison offence, investigate the charge and punish the prisoner if found guilty for up to 30 days confinement and 24 strokes of the cane.
* May under the Societies Act 1966, enter or authorize any police officer to enter any place if they believe that a meeting of an unlawful society is being held there. They may also issue a search warrant to seize any items connected to the unlawful society and warrant of arrest on any persons found in that place.
* Administer oaths under the Trade Marks Act 1998, Merchant Shipping Act 1995 and Parliamentary Elections Act 1954 and Presidential Elections Act 1991.

Other roles of a JP includes being mediators in the [State Courts of Singapore](/wiki/State_Courts_of_Singapore "State Courts of Singapore") or marriage solemnisers in the [Registry of Marriages](/wiki/Registry_of_Marriages "Registry of Marriages").[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Newly appointed justices of the peace are required by Section 17 of the State Courts Act to take the oath of office and allegiance as set out in the schedule to the State Courts Act, before exercising the functions of their good office. The President may also revoke the appointment of any Justice of the Peace.

### Sri Lanka

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Sri Lanka")]

In Sri Lanka, Justice of the Peace is an honorary post, with authorisation to witness and sign [statutory declarations](/wiki/Statutory_declaration "Statutory declaration") and [affidavits](/wiki/Affidavit "Affidavit") as well as [certify documents](/wiki/Certified_copy "Certified copy"). Persons appointed as a Justice of the Peace may use the post-nominal **JP**. Current appointments are made under the *Judicature Act No 02 of 1978*, by the [Minister of Justice](/wiki/Minister_of_Justice_(Sri_Lanka) "Minister of Justice (Sri Lanka)") at his/her discretion by publishing a list in *[The Gazette](/wiki/The_Sri_Lanka_Gazette "The Sri Lanka Gazette")* and appointee taking oaths before a [high court](/wiki/High_court "High court"), [district court](/wiki/District_court "District court") [judge](/wiki/Judge "Judge") or [magistrate](/wiki/Magistrate "Magistrate") with [registrar](/wiki/Registrar_(law) "Registrar (law)") of the [supreme court](/wiki/Supreme_court "Supreme court") recording it. There are four types of appointments of Justice of the Peace;

* [Justice of the Peace and Unofficial magistrate](/wiki/Unofficial_magistrate "Unofficial magistrate")
* Justice of the Peace for the Whole Island
* Justice of the Peace for a Judicial District
* Justice of the Peace (ex officio)

Senior [Attorney at laws](/wiki/Attorney_at_law "Attorney at law") are appointed as [Justice of the Peace and Unofficial magistrates](/wiki/Unofficial_magistrate "Unofficial magistrate") to preside in the absence of a sitting [Magistrate](/wiki/Magistrate "Magistrate").[[54]](#cite_note-54) Any citizen of [Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka") can apply to the Ministry of Justice giving his or her credentials to be appointed as a justice of the peace. However, the applicant should be one who has served the public and carries out social service and should be of good standing. These JPs would be appointed with legal authority in all parts of the island or limited to a judicial district. The [President of Sri Lanka](/wiki/President_of_Sri_Lanka "President of Sri Lanka") and his/her officers are *[ex officio](/wiki/Ex_officio_member "Ex officio member")* justices of the peace. There about 100,000 JPs in the island.[[55]](#cite_note-ft-55)

The post was introduced in the island during the British colonial era by the Governor [Frederick North](/wiki/Frederick_North,_5th_Earl_of_Guilford "Frederick North, 5th Earl of Guilford") in 1801 and was later reformed in 1871.[[55]](#cite_note-ft-55) Until 1938, appointments were made by the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Ceylon "Governor of Ceylon"), after which appointments were made by the Legal Secretary until 1947. After Ceylon gained its independence in 1948, appointments were made by the [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Ceylon "Governor General of Ceylon") and the [Minister of Justice](/wiki/Minister_of_Justice_(Sri_Lanka) "Minister of Justice (Sri Lanka)"). Justice of the Peace had the power to administer oaths and affirmations per the *Courts Ordinance No. 1 on 1889 section 84* and they could formally appoint members of the public to act as [special police officers](/wiki/Special_police "Special police") in times of turmoil and riots. Since certain government officers were ex-officio justices of the peace, this allowed British colonial officers to appoint special police officers from the European planters in times of crisis such as the [1915 riots](/wiki/1915_Ceylonese_riots "1915 Ceylonese riots").[[56]](#cite_note-56) The Village Councils Law (No. 6 of 1964) made the Chairman of the Village Council an *ex officio* justices of the peace for that village area.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Tonga

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Tonga")]

In 2014, for the first time, Justices of the Peace were authorised in [Tonga](/wiki/Tonga "Tonga"). JPs are appointed by the Crown, but the Lord Chief Justice regulates their duties and defines their powers. The first JPs were warranted with duties including granting bail; issuing search warrants and subpoenas; taking affidavits, declarations and oaths; and having the power to witness documents. Term of office is one year and officials can be reappointed. The initial 19 JPs appointed were: ‘Aisea Ta’ofi and Sione Hinakau of [Niuatoputapu](/wiki/Niuatoputapu "Niuatoputapu"); ‘Inoke Tuaimei’api of [Niuafo'ou](/wiki/Niuafo%27ou "Niuafo'ou"); Siosiua Hausia from [ʻEua](/wiki/%CA%BBEua "ʻEua"); Sione Palu, Sione Fakahua, Me’ite Fukofuka and Kisione Taulani of [Ha’apai](/wiki/Ha%E2%80%99apai "Ha’apai"); Salesi Kauvaka, Viliami Pasikala, Haniteli Fa’anunu, Meli Taufaeteau and Moleni Taufa from [Vava’u](/wiki/Vava%E2%80%99u "Vava’u"); and Salote Fukofuka, ‘Amelia Helu, [‘Ofa Likiliki](/wiki/%E2%80%98Ofa_Likiliki "‘Ofa Likiliki"), Tevita Fakatou, Sioape Tu’iono and Semisi Tongia of [Tongatapu](/wiki/Tongatapu "Tongatapu").[[58]](#cite_note-Tonga_Broadcasting-58)

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

#### England and Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: England and Wales")]

Main article: [Magistrate (England and Wales)](/wiki/Magistrate_(England_and_Wales) "Magistrate (England and Wales)")

A [magistrates' court](/wiki/Magistrates%27_court_(England_and_Wales) "Magistrates' court (England and Wales)") in [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") is typically composed of a [bench](/wiki/Bench_(law) "Bench (law)") of (usually three) Justices of the Peace (otherwise known as [magistrates](/wiki/Magistrate#English_common_law_tradition "Magistrate")) who dispense [summary justice](/wiki/Summary_offence "Summary offence"). They decide on offences which carry a community sentence, a prison sentence (maximum of six months for any one offence, up to one year for multiple offences), or an unlimited fine.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60) They are advised on points of law and procedure by a legally qualified [justices' clerk](/wiki/Justices%27_clerk "Justices' clerk") and their assistants.[[61]](#cite_note-61) In practice, JPs have a wide range of sentencing options, which include issuing fines, imposing [community orders](/wiki/Community_service "Community service"), or dealing with offences by means of a [discharge](/wiki/Discharge_(sentence) "Discharge (sentence)"). In more serious cases, where magistrates' consider that their sentencing powers are insufficient, they can send '[either-way](/wiki/Hybrid_offence "Hybrid offence")' offenders to the [Crown Court](/wiki/Crown_Court "Crown Court") for sentencing.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Justices of the Peace are trained volunteers.[[63]](#cite_note-Become_a_magistrate-63) No formal qualifications are required but magistrates need intelligence, common sense, integrity and the capacity to act fairly.[[63]](#cite_note-Become_a_magistrate-63) Membership is widely spread throughout the local area and drawn from all walks of life following a rigorous selection process undertaken by a local advisory committee, who recommends to the [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") those individuals who have demonstrated the five key qualities for appointment which are: (1) awareness of social issues; (2) maturity and understanding; (3) reliability and commitment; (4) understanding of documents and effective communication; and (5) logical thinking.[[64]](#cite_note-64) Justices of the Peace are trained volunteers, but those who are employed in some occupations (e.g. Police Officers) cannot be appointed due to potential conflict of interest.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

All new Justices of the Peace undergo comprehensive training before sitting. There is a mentoring program to help guide new appointees (mentors are magistrates with at least three years' service[[66]](#cite_note-66)). The training is delivered by the [Judicial College](/wiki/Judicial_College "Judicial College") and covers the necessary law and procedure required for their role. They continue to receive training throughout their judicial career, and are appraised every four years (every two years for a Presiding Justice) to check that they continue to remain competent in their role.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Additional training is given to justices choosing to sit in the Youth Court, or those dealing with family matters. New JPs sit with mentors on at least six occasions during their first eighteen months. Justices of the Peace are unpaid appointees, but they may receive allowances to cover travelling expenses, subsistence, and loss of earnings for those not paid by their employer while sitting as a magistrate, up to £116.78 a day. Such person may sit at any magistrates' court in England and Wales, but in practice they are appointed to their local bench (a colloquial and legal term for the local court). Justices of the Peace often sit as a panel of three; two as a minimum in most cases, save for cases under the Single Justice Procedure. Many are members of the Magistrates' Association, which provides advice, training and represents magistrates.[[68]](#cite_note-68) Justices of the Peace must sit for a minimum of 26 sessions (half-days) per year.[[69]](#cite_note-gov.uk-69) An employer must, by law, allow a Justice of the Peace reasonable time off work to serve as such.[[69]](#cite_note-gov.uk-69)[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

The lead magistrate is known as a Presiding Justice (PJ) and should be addressed in court as "sir" or "ma'am" or "your worship", and the magistrates collectively as "your worships". In writing they are their usual name followed by "JP" (for Justice of the Peace).[[72]](#cite_note-72) Other magistrates on the bench are known as "wingers".[[73]](#cite_note-magistrates-association.org.uk-73) All three magistrates contribute equally to the decision-making and carry equal authority, but the Presiding Justice will speak on their behalf in open court.[[73]](#cite_note-magistrates-association.org.uk-73)

Magistrates' courts today can deal with lesser offences such as all summary offences, and some more serious triable '[either-way](/wiki/Hybrid_offence "Hybrid offence")' matters, but where the magistrates' deem that their sentencing powers are sufficient. However, all criminal cases start in the magistrates' court. They handle over 95% of the criminal cases in [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") and [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland").[[74]](#cite_note-74) With more serious offences, magistrates are responsible for indictment and committal to the [Crown Court](/wiki/Crown_Court "Crown Court") (a task in former times dealt with by a [grand jury](/wiki/Grand_jury "Grand jury")). Magistrates also have a civil [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), such as a family jurisdiction, or appeals against matters relating to licensing. Although they had a licensing jurisdiction dealing liquor, betting and clubs licensing applications, this was transferred under the [Licensing Act 2003](/wiki/Licensing_Act_2003 "Licensing Act 2003") to local authorities. The magistrates now act in licensing matters only as an appeal court from the decisions of the local authority. Justices of the Peace are responsible for granting orders such as search warrants to the Police and other authorities. They were required to live within 15 miles of where they sit in case they were needed to sign a warrant after hours, but they were replaced with *Local Justice Areas* by the [Courts Act 2003](/wiki/Courts_Act_2003 "Courts Act 2003"), meaning this is not formally required. Section 7 of the Courts Act 2003 states that "There shall be a commission of the peace for England and Wales—...b) addressed generally, and not by name, to all such persons as may from time to time hold office as justices of the peace for England and Wales". Thus, every magistrate in England and Wales may act as a magistrate anywhere there.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

Cardiff Magistrates' Court is the only court in the country which deals with offences under the [Companies Act 2006](/wiki/Companies_Act_2006 "Companies Act 2006"), such as for late filing of accounts or directors' offences. [Westminster Magistrates' Court](/wiki/Westminster_Magistrates%27_Court "Westminster Magistrates' Court") has special responsibilities for dealing with terrorism and extradition offences throughout the UK.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

The [Courts Act 2003](/wiki/Courts_Act_2003 "Courts Act 2003") provides the current framework for appointment of the justices, which is done by the [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") in the name of [sovereign](/wiki/British_monarchy "British monarchy"). Justices can also be removed by the same mechanism.[[77]](#cite_note-77)

Before 1714, magistrates were liable to be approached at any time and in any place by people legally recognised as [paupers](/wiki/Pauper "Pauper"), appealing for aid if [parish authorities](/wiki/Vestry "Vestry") refused to provide any. It was relatively common for these magistrates to write out, on the spot, an order requiring aid to be granted.[[78]](#cite_note-historyandpolicy-78) The magistracy is an ancient institution, dating in England from at least 1327. The role is underpinned by the principles of 'local justice' and 'justice by one's peers.[[79]](#cite_note-79)'

As at 2021, 56% of sitting magistrates were women, 13% were black, Asian and minority ethnic, and 82% aged above 50 as at 1 April 2021.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

#### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Scotland")]

See also: [Judiciary of Scotland](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Scotland "Judiciary of Scotland")

Within the Scottish legal system Justices of the Peace are trained volunteers who currently sit in the [Justice of the Peace courts](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace_court "Justice of the peace court"). These courts were introduced in 2009 as a replacement for the [district courts](/wiki/District_court_(Scotland) "District court (Scotland)") (established in 1975), which in turn replaced burgh police courts.[[81]](#cite_note-districtCourtsScotland-81) Justices sit alone or in threes with a qualified legal assessor as convener or clerk of court. They handle many cases of breaches of the peace – drunkenness, minor assaults, petty theft, and offences under the [Civic Government (Scotland) Act 1982](/wiki/Civic_Government_(Scotland)_Act_1982 "Civic Government (Scotland) Act 1982").[[82]](#cite_note-civicGovernemtnAct-82)

The maximum sentencing power of a justice of the peace is 60 days [imprisonment](/wiki/Imprisonment "Imprisonment"), or a fine up to £2,500, or both, and the ability to disqualify drivers.[[83]](#cite_note-jppowers-83)

In 2006, the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") announced its intention to unify the management of the sheriff and district courts in Scotland but retain lay justices, as part of its initiative to create a unified judiciary under the [Lord President](/wiki/Lord_President_of_the_Court_of_Session "Lord President of the Court of Session"). Following the passage of the Criminal Proceedings etc. (Reform) (Scotland) Act 2007 the justice of the peace courts were implemented on a [sheriffdom](/wiki/Sheriffdom "Sheriffdom")-by-sheriffdom basis.[[84]](#cite_note-jp2007-84): Section 59

In Glasgow, the volume of business required the employment of three solicitors as "stipendiary magistrates" who sat in place of the lay justices. The stipendiary magistrates' court had the same sentencing power as the summary [sheriff court](/wiki/Sheriff_court "Sheriff court") in [summary proceedings](/wiki/Summary_offence "Summary offence"), which was the ability to [sentence](/wiki/Sentence_(law) "Sentence (law)") an offender to up to one year in [prison](/wiki/Imprisonment "Imprisonment") or [fine](/wiki/Fine_(penalty) "Fine (penalty)") them up to [£](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling")10,000. Stipendiary magistrates were replaced by summary sheriffs.[[85]](#cite_note-ScottishCriminalCourts-85)[[86]](#cite_note-summarysheriff-86)

#### Northern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Northern Ireland")]

In Northern Ireland, the situation initially continued as it had in pre-1922 Ireland. However, justices of the peace no longer sat out of petty sessions after 1935 (Summary Jurisdiction and Criminal Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 1935).[[87]](#cite_note-87) Since then, magistrates' courts in Northern Ireland have consisted of legally qualified resident magistrates (now known as district judges (magistrates' courts)) sitting alone, except in cases involving children, where two lay panelists sat with the magistrate. Justices of the peace were confined to the power to conduct committal hearings, bind persons over to the peace, sign warrants, summons, and other official documents. They were appointed by the [Lord Chancellor](/wiki/Lord_Chancellor "Lord Chancellor") on the recommendation of a committee in each county court division.

The [Justice (Northern Ireland) Act 2002](/wiki/Justice_(Northern_Ireland)_Act_2002 "Justice (Northern Ireland) Act 2002") introduced a new office of lay magistrate, to sit alongside resident magistrates at magistrates' courts in certain matters. Unlike in England and Wales, "lay magistrate" is the official title of the position, to distinguish from existing justices of the peace who do not sit in the magistrates' courts. The first lay magistrates were appointed in 2005. Two lay magistrates sit with the district judge (magistrates' court) in criminal proceedings involving children (replacing the former lay panelists) and Family Proceedings Court matters. The district judge (magistrates' court), who is a barrister or solicitor of at least seven years standing, presides over the bench.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Most criminal justice functions of JPs were transferred to lay magistrates. It is expected that there will be no further appointments of justices of the peace in Northern Ireland, although those already appointed retain the title and any functions not transferred to lay magistrate under the 2002 Act.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: United States")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2a/Justice_of_the_Peace_Sturgeon_Bay.jpg/250px-Justice_of_the_Peace_Sturgeon_Bay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Justice_of_the_Peace_Sturgeon_Bay.jpg)

Justice of the Peace office in [Sturgeon Bay, Wisconsin](/wiki/Sturgeon_Bay,_Wisconsin "Sturgeon Bay, Wisconsin")

In some US states, the justice of the peace is a judge of a [court](/wiki/Court "Court") of limited [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction"), a [magistrate](/wiki/Magistrate "Magistrate"), or a quasi-judicial official with certain statutory or common law magisterial powers.[[89]](#cite_note-89) Some states have special qualifications or unique features for the office.

The justice of the peace typically presides over a court that hears [misdemeanor](/wiki/Misdemeanor "Misdemeanor") cases, traffic violations, and other petty criminal infractions. The justice of the peace may also have authority over cases involving small [debts](/wiki/Debt "Debt"), [landlord](/wiki/Landlord "Landlord") and [tenant](/wiki/Leasehold_estate "Leasehold estate") disputes, or other [small claims court](/wiki/Small_claims_court "Small claims court") proceedings. Proceedings before justices of the peace are often faster and less formal than the proceedings in other courts. In some jurisdictions a party convicted or found liable before a justice of the peace may have the right to a [trial de novo](/wiki/Trial_de_novo "Trial de novo") before the judge of a higher court rather than an appeal strictly considered.

A justice of the peace also performs [civil marriages](/wiki/Civil_marriage "Civil marriage").

#### Arizona

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Arizona")]

A justice of the peace has the same jurisdiction as a municipal magistrate with respect to traffic and [misdemeanor](/wiki/Misdemeanor "Misdemeanor") cases and restraining orders, though over cases whose affairs are not contained within the confines of a single municipality. Additionally, the Justice Court hears cases involving county ordinances (ordinances enacted by the board of supervisors that apply only to unincorporated areas), civil lawsuits up to a limit of $10,000, small claims cases up to $2,500 (up to $3,500 in Maricopa County[[90]](#cite_note-90)), and issues evictions, called writs of restitution (after a forcible detainer or special detainer action (eviction) being successfully completed by a landlord). Justices of the peace, also called JPs, or Judges of the Justice Court, are elected in partisan elections for four-year terms from specific districts called precincts. They have the same authority and responsibility as all other judges in the state with respect to performing marriages, administering oaths, adhering to the code of judicial conduct, and all aspects of justice administration. However, Arizona law does not require justices of the peace to be lawyers. Many justices of the peace are not legally trained, although all are required by the Arizona Supreme Court to complete a course at the Arizona Judicial College. As with JPs, municipal judges in Arizona are not required to be lawyers.

In [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"), a justice of the peace is an elected official equivalent to a [county commissioner](/wiki/County_commissioner "County commissioner") or county supervisor in some other states. Arkansas JPs sit on a county quorum court, composed of 9, 11, 13 or 15 JPs. The quorum court is a part-time body, elected from single-member districts, that has overall responsibility for county affairs. Among their responsibilities are passing the budget, creating new ordinances (at the misdemeanor level), setting property tax millage levels, and working with other elected officials. The full-time elected county administrator, who presides over the quorum court, is the county judge. Neither JPs nor the county judge have any judicial authority, though they do have the power to preside over civil marriages. Justices of the peace are elected every two years to these partisan offices. JPs first gained formal legislative authority under constitutional Amendment 55, passed by voters in 1974.

Justices of the peace in Connecticut can preside over [marriages](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage"). Unlike some states, Connecticut JPs are not penalized for refusing to perform such ceremonies. They have the same general oath-giving powers as a [notary public](/wiki/Notary_public "Notary public").

Florida had justices of the peace (with corresponding constables) from the time of its acquisition from Spain in 1821 until the Florida Constitution was amended in 1968 to abolish the post. From about 1940 to 1968, Florida counties had the ability to hold local referendums to allow county voters to abolish the post on a county-by-county basis. For example, Leon County, the location of Tallahassee, Florida's capital city, voted to abolish justices of the peace (and their associated constables) in the Fall elections of 1958. By 1958, the county commission had reduced the number of JOP districts from a turn-of-the-century peak of 13 districts to just two districts. The automobile age made the county sheriff able to patrol the entire county and made it possible for the citizenry to travel to the courthouse for legal proceedings.

#### Louisiana

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Louisiana")]

Justices of the peace in Louisiana are elected to serve six-year terms as the judicial authority of a ward or district, but not where city courts exist. They have jurisdiction in civil matters when the amount in dispute does not exceed $5,000. They do not have jurisdiction when a title to real estate is involved, when the state or any political subdivision is a defendant, or in successions or probate matters. They are authorized to perform marriage ceremonies. There are around 390 such Justices of the peace.[[91]](#cite_note-lasc.libguides-91)[[92]](#cite_note-Justia-92)

#### Maine

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Maine")]

In [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine"), the office of the Justice of the Peace was merged with [Notary Public](/wiki/Notary_Public "Notary Public"), a process that took place from 1981 to 1988.[[93]](#cite_note-Maine-93) The duties that were attached to the office of Justice of the Peace were fully transferred to the Notary Public in 1988. The office currently named as "Justice of the Peace" in Maine is a court officer, an attorney, involved in the process of issuing warrants and responding to complaints. The office however is not related to either the current Notary Public office or the previous Justice of the Peace office and is part of the [Maine Bureau of Corporations, Elections & Commissions](/w/index.php?title=Maine_Bureau_of_Corporations,_Elections_%26_Commissions&action=edit&redlink=1 "Maine Bureau of Corporations, Elections & Commissions (page does not exist)").[[93]](#cite_note-Maine-93)

#### Massachusetts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Justice_of_the_peace&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Massachusetts")]

Justices of the peace in the Commonwealth of Massachusetts are commissioned by the [Governor](/wiki/Governor_of_Massachusetts "Governor of Massachusetts") with the advice and consent of the [Council](/wiki/Massachusetts_Governor%27s_Council "Massachusetts Governor's Council") for seven-year terms. They are often called on to solemnize non-religious marriages, especially [same-sex marriages](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage"), which certain religious officials are not willing to oversee. They have the same general oath-giving powers as a [notary public](/wiki/Notary_public "Notary public"), and are also empowered to issue certain writs. Justices of peace are also empowered to keep the peace and suppress riots, and enforce all laws that keep public peace.[[94]](#cite_note-94) Furthermore, upon the recommendation of a town selectman, they may be authorized by the governor to take bail in criminal cases.[[95]](#cite_note-95)
